3) User clicks the in-page navigation link to the free shipping section, which updates the URL to shipping.html?section=free_shipping and scrolls to the corresponding section.
4) In the free shipping section, policy text from getShippingAndReturnsContent.free_shipping_criteria is displayed, and explanatory static labels clarify that eligible products are flagged by the Product.free_shipping attribute and can be filtered in product listings using the “Free shipping” filter.
5) User may copy or note the criteria, then uses browser back or header navigation to continue shopping.

Workflow 3: Preparing or requesting a product return
1) User wants to return or exchange a product and either selects a link from help.html or navigates directly to shipping.html?section=returns.
2) On load, the page reads section=returns, fetches policy content and shipping methods, and scrolls directly to the returns policy section.
3) User reviews returns_policy and restocking_fees from getShippingAndReturnsContent to understand timing, conditions, and any fees.
4) If the user still has questions or requires an exception, they click the “Contact us about a return” link.
5) The click navigates to contact.html with topic=returns so the contact form can be pre‑filled or pre-filtered for returns-related inquiries.

Workflow 4: Checking delivery coverage and methods for specific regions
1) User navigates to shipping.html, either from the footer or via help.html.
2) The page loads all interfaces and shows the overview at the top.
3) User clicks the in-page navigation link for delivery areas, which updates the URL (section=delivery_areas) and scrolls to that section.
4) The delivery_areas field from getShippingAndReturnsContent is shown, explaining geographic coverage and any region-specific notes (without hardcoding any regions in the UI logic).
5) User then clicks the in-page navigation link for shipping methods (section=methods) to view all ShippingMethod entries returned by getShippingMethods.
6) User reads information for each method (cost, estimated days, description) and then decides whether to proceed with checkout or contact support.
interactions: Clicking an in-page navigation item updates the URL section parameter via pushState/replaceState and triggers a scroll to the corresponding section container.
Changing the section parameter via browser navigation (back/forward) triggers the section-scroll handler to realign the view with the currently selected section.

On initial page load, a lifecycle event triggers calls to getShippingAndReturnsContent, getShippingMethods, and getCartSummary; components bind to the returned data instead of any static or hardcoded shipping information.
Clicking “View FAQs about shipping” navigates to help.html with the query parameter section=shipping.
Clicking “Contact us about a return” navigates to contact.html with the query parameter topic=returns.
Clicking “Back to checkout” navigates to checkout.html without carrying over the section parameter, letting checkout rebuild its own state.

If any interface call fails or returns no data, components display generic error or fallback messages that do not attempt to guess shipping or returns details; these messages are purely structural (e.g., informing the user that data is unavailable).

The cart summary indicator is clickable (if desired) and may navigate the user to the cart or checkout page, but this page itself does not alter cart contents; it only displays the read-only summary from getCartSummary.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver for this page is the URL query parameter `section`. Supported values are identifiers mapped to specific sections: for example `overview`, `free_shipping`, `delivery_areas`, `methods`, `returns`, and `restocking_fees`. On page load:
1) The page parses `section` from the URL (if present).
2) The page concurrently calls getShippingAndReturnsContent, getShippingMethods, and getCartSummary.
3) Once content is ready, a central section-scroll handler checks the current `section` value:
   - If it matches a known identifier, the page scrolls to the corresponding section container (by DOM id or anchor).
   - If it is missing or unrecognized, the page defaults to the top/overview section without inferring any policy details.
4) When the user clicks an in-page navigation link, the handler updates the `section` parameter in the URL (using history.pushState or history.replaceState) and immediately scrolls to the associated section element, without re-calling the interfaces.
5) When the browser's back/forward buttons change the URL's `section` parameter (popstate event), the section-scroll handler re-runs and scrolls the page so that the displayed section matches the URL, again without refetching data.
6) Interface data is not cached in URL parameters; only the section anchor is encoded. All shipping, free shipping, returns, and fee details are always sourced from the latest responses of getShippingAndReturnsContent and getShippingMethods to avoid conflicts with static or hardcoded values.

components.functionality: Displays the functional page title 'Shipping & Returns' and, if needed, a short static subtitle describing that the page covers shipping methods, free shipping, and returns policies.
Shows a compact cart summary (item count and subtotal) in the header or a fixed area so users can see their current cart state while reading shipping and returns information.
event_handlers: onLoad: Call getCartSummary and bind the returned item_count, subtotal, and currency to the display fields.
onClick: Optionally navigate the user to checkout.html or cart.html (navigation behavior defined globally; this component only emits a navigation event, without altering cart data).

components.functionality: Provides a list of links or buttons to jump between sections of the page: overview, free shipping, delivery areas, shipping methods, returns, and restocking fees. Helps users quickly reach relevant information and keeps the URL in sync via the section parameter.
event_handlers: onClick: When a navigation item is clicked, update the URL query parameter `section` to the corresponding section identifier (e.g., free_shipping, returns) using history.pushState or history.replaceState, and call the section-scroll handler to scroll to that section.
onLoad: Read the current `section` parameter and highlight the matching navigation item as active without re-fetching any data.

components.functionality: Centralizes logic for reading the `section` parameter and scrolling the viewport to the matching section container. Keeps the visible section and URL synchronized without using localStorage.
event_handlers: onLoad: After interfaces finish loading and section containers are rendered, read `section` from the URL; if it matches a known section (e.g., overview, free_shipping, delivery_areas, methods, returns, restocking_fees), scroll to that section; otherwise, leave at the top.
onPopState: When the browser back/forward navigation changes the URL, re-read `section` and scroll to the new active section.
onSectionNavClick: Invoked by section-navigation when a user clicks a navigation item; updates URL via pushState/replaceState and scrolls to the section’s DOM element.

components.functionality: Fetches policy text for shipping and returns using getShippingAndReturnsContent and provides it to content sections via a shared state or props, ensuring no policy text is hardcoded in the UI.
event_handlers: onLoad: Call getShippingAndReturnsContent and store the resulting object (with shipping_overview, free_shipping_criteria, delivery_areas, returns_policy, restocking_fees) in page state for use by section components.
onError: If the call fails, expose a flag to dependent sections so they can show a generic error message rather than fabricated policy details.

components.functionality: Displays the general shipping overview text, such as explanation of processing times and general conditions, using data from getShippingAndReturnsContent.shipping_overview.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When shipping_overview is updated by shipping-returns-content-loader, render the text within this section.
onErrorFlag: If the loader indicates an error, show a neutral message that shipping overview information is temporarily unavailable.

components.functionality: Shows the detailed criteria for free shipping and explains how these criteria relate to product listings and filters (e.g., how the Product.free_shipping attribute is used), using the free_shipping_criteria field from getShippingAndReturnsContent.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When free_shipping_criteria is available, inject the returned text into this section.

onExplainFilter: Optionally respond to a help tooltip click by showing a small non-policy explanation of how free_shipping is displayed (e.g., as a badge or filter), without modifying any product data.

components.functionality: Presents delivery coverage and area-related shipping information, using the delivery_areas field from getShippingAndReturnsContent.
event_handlers: onDataChange: Render the delivery_areas text when provided by the content loader.
onErrorFlag: If data cannot be loaded, display a basic notice that delivery area details are not available, without assuming any regions.

components.functionality: Fetches available shipping methods via getShippingMethods and exposes the list of ShippingMethod entities for display.
event_handlers: onLoad: Call getShippingMethods and store the returned array for use by the shipping-methods-section.
onError: If fetching shipping methods fails, set an error state so the section can inform the user that methods cannot be displayed at this time.

components.functionality: Displays all shipping methods returned by getShippingMethods, including each method’s name, description, base_cost, estimated delivery range, and whether it can be a free option under certain conditions.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When shipping-methods-loader provides data, sort methods by display_order (if present) and render a row for each ShippingMethod using only fields from the API.
onRowHover: Optionally show a tooltip with additional information from ShippingMethod.description, without altering underlying data.
onErrorFlag: If shipping-methods-loader indicates an error, display a structural error message instead of any assumed or static shipping method list.

components.functionality: Shows the detailed returns and exchanges policy text using returns_policy from getShippingAndReturnsContent, including conditions, timing, and steps as defined by the backend content.
event_handlers: onDataChange: Render the returns_policy text when provided by the content loader.
onContactLinkClick: Emit an event that triggers navigation via the contact-returns-link component when the user chooses to contact support about a return.

components.functionality: Displays information on any restocking fees related to returns, using restocking_fees from getShippingAndReturnsContent, without hardcoding amounts or percentages in the UI.
event_handlers: onDataChange: Render restocking_fees text when updated by the content loader.
onErrorFlag: If no restocking information is available, show a structural message that restocking fee information is currently unavailable.

components.functionality: Provides a direct link from the Shipping & Returns page to the shipping-related FAQs section on help.html.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate to help.html with the query parameter section=shipping, ensuring the FAQs page opens directly at the shipping section.

components.functionality: Provides a direct link for users to contact support about returns, pre-filtering the contact topic.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate to contact.html with the query parameter topic=returns so that the contact page can show or preselect returns-related options.

components.functionality: Allows users who arrived from checkout or cart pages to return to the checkout process after reviewing shipping and returns information.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate directly to checkout.html without adding section or other query parameters from this page, letting checkout reconstruct its own state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid typical of WooCommerce/WordPress themes, with content centered in a fixed-width container inside a full-bleed header.
layout.layout_pattern: Replicate the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure: full‑width site header at the very top (black utility bar + yellow primary bar + search/category band) using the existing page-header and cart-summary-indicator. Immediately beneath, the main Shipping & Returns page content sits in a centered, fixed‑width container: a breadcrumb (assumed inside page-header or global layout), then a spacious gap, then a large centered H1 title for Shipping & Returns, followed by a single-row horizontal section-navigation bar (mirroring the product category filter row from the mockup). Below this navigation, all content sections (overview, free shipping, delivery areas, shipping methods table, returns policy, restocking fees) are stacked vertically in a single main column, left‑aligned, with generous spacing between them. The three navigation links (shipping-faq-link, contact-returns-link, back-to-checkout-link) are placed as contextual navigation near the top-right of the content and as a prominent exit path at the bottom, without disturbing the core vertical reading flow. Invisible behavior/data components (section-scroll-handler, loaders) sit logically around these sections without occupying visual space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Replicate the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure: full‑width site header at the very top (black utility bar + yellow primary bar + search/category band) using the existing page-header and cart-summary-indicator. Immediately beneath, the main Shipping & Returns page content sits in a centered, fixed‑width container: a breadcrumb (assumed inside page-header or global layout), then a spacious gap, then a large centered H1 title for Shipping & Returns, followed by a single-row horizontal section-navigation bar (mirroring the product category filter row from the mockup). Below this navigation, all content sections (overview, free shipping, delivery areas, shipping methods table, returns policy, restocking fees) are stacked vertically in a single main column, left‑aligned, with generous spacing between them. The three navigation links (shipping-faq-link, contact-returns-link, back-to-checkout-link) are placed as contextual navigation near the top-right of the content and as a prominent exit path at the bottom, without disturbing the core vertical reading flow. Invisible behavior/data components (section-scroll-handler, loaders) sit logically around these sections without occupying visual space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header band (page-header with cart-summary-indicator and the search/category band) occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the initial viewport, full width, but with core content constrained to a central container. The introductory midsection—title + section-navigation + top help links—occupies another ~20–25% with generous vertical spacing, echoing the open feel of the mockup’s title + category filter area. The lower ~50–60% of the page is dedicated to stacked informational sections and the shipping-methods table, whose content density increases progressively as the user scrolls down. Horizontally, all main page content lives inside a central container (about 70–80% of viewport width on desktop, e.g., 1140px max), centered with equal left/right margins, mirroring the product grid region from the mockup. Section headings and body text generally span about 70–80% of this container’s width, keeping line lengths readable while maintaining airy margins. Navigation links at the top and bottom occupy narrow horizontal bands and small portions of width, acting as accents rather than major blocks.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density follows the mockup’s gradient: low density in the header and introductory region, higher density in the detailed sections. Above the fold, individual elements (title, section-navigation, help links) are separated by 24–40px vertical gaps, with only a few interactive elements visible at once, maintaining a calm, corporate tone. Each content section has its own heading and short paragraphs with 16–24px line spacing, leaving plenty of whitespace between sections (32–48px). The shipping-methods-section is the densest part of the page, similar to the product grid: tabular data is compact but still aligned to the 8px spacing system, with comfortable row height and column padding to avoid overwhelming the user. Overall, the layout remains clean and breathable, never showing more than one or two dense components in the viewport at the same time.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the Z‑pattern from the mockup. On page load, the eye starts top-left at the logo and main nav in the page-header, moves right toward the cart-summary-indicator and account icons, then diagonally down-left into the main content container where the Shipping & Returns title sits. From there, attention moves right across the section-navigation row and the small help links (shipping-faq-link and contact-returns-link). As the user scrolls, the vertical flow becomes dominant: the eye moves down Section by Section—overview, free shipping, delivery areas, methods table, returns, restocking—similar to scanning a product grid, but in a single column. Back-to-checkout-link at the bottom-left acts as the final focal point, providing a clear exit path back to the transactional flow. Invisible behaviors (section-scroll-handler) support this flow by smoothly scrolling to sections and optionally pinning the in-page navigation in view, keeping orientation without adding visual clutter.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a structured, corporate e‑commerce help page that visually echoes a product listing layout. A full-width header spans the top, with a slim black utility strip above a bold yellow navigation bar containing the logo on the left and cart/account actions on the right. Directly beneath is a horizontal search and category band that stretches across the viewport, anchoring the top 20–25% of the screen. Below this, the main content sits in a centered, fixed-width container with generous side margins. After a subtle breadcrumb row, a large, centered “Shipping & Returns” title appears with ample vertical breathing room above and below. Just underneath, a single-line horizontal navigation row lists the key subsections (Overview, Free Shipping, Delivery Areas, Shipping Methods, Returns & Fees) in evenly spaced text links, similar in treatment and position to the category filter in the mockup. To the right side of this band, lighter-weight text links for FAQs and contacting returns support quick help actions without pulling focus from the main nav. Scrolling downward, the layout becomes a tall, left-aligned vertical stack of content sections, each with a clear heading and concise copy, separated by generous white space. The shipping methods appear in a denser, well-structured table that echoes the product grid’s functional clarity. At the very bottom of the main content column, a prominent “Back to Checkout” style link, aligned to the left, provides a clear way back into the purchase flow. The overall impression is clean, breathable, and highly legible, with a familiar e‑commerce rhythm adapted from the product listing mockup to a policy/guide context.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-header occupies the very top of the viewport, spanning 100% of the browser width as a full-bleed band. It is composed of three vertically stacked horizontal strips: (1) a thin black utility bar at the very top (~40px tall) containing secondary links and possibly contact info, logo aligned left; (2) directly below, a taller yellow main header bar (~72–88px tall) that aligns with the implicit 12-column grid and houses the primary logo at the far left and main navigation links across the center-left, with the cart-summary-indicator and account icons grouped at the far right; and (3) immediately beneath, a horizontal search/category band (~64px tall) that sits on white or lightly tinted background, containing a left-aligned “Browse Categories” button and a central search input that together span roughly 70% of the fixed-width inner container. The header’s content is constrained within a centered container (approximately 1140px wide, or ~70–80% of viewport width), with even side margins; background colors extend full-bleed to the edges of the screen. Vertically, the entire page-header block consumes about the top quarter of the first screen, establishing strong brand presence before transitioning into the main page content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow: it sits above all other components and scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page (no sticky behavior).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-indicator is placed inside the yellow main header bar, aligned to the far right within the centered header container. It appears on the same horizontal row as the primary navigation links, visually grouped with account/login icons. The indicator itself occupies a small rectangular area—roughly the width of 1–2 grid columns—showing a cart icon and item count. It sits flush with the top and bottom padding of the header bar, with 16–24px of horizontal padding between it and neighboring icons/links. On wide screens, it visually anchors the top-right corner of the page, forming the endpoint of the primary left-to-right scan across the header. Its position is consistent across pages, aligning with the right edge of the content container beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inside the page-header; it moves off-screen together with the header as the user scrolls and does not float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-navigation sits within the main content container, below the header and after the page title. After the header scrolls, there is a breadcrumb row (part of the global template) followed by a vertical gap of about 32–40px, then the Shipping & Returns H1 centered in the container. Another 24–32px of whitespace below the title leads into the section-navigation, which is rendered as a single horizontal strip approximately 48–56px tall. This strip spans the full width of the inner content container (around 70–80% of viewport width) and is centered on the page, with text links representing each major section (“Overview”, “Free Shipping”, “Delivery Areas”, “Shipping Methods”, “Returns & Fees”). The links are laid out on one line, evenly spaced or center-aligned, with 24–32px between labels. The overall row is visually similar to the category filter row in the mockup: low vertical density, clear typography, and possibly an underline or highlight under the active section. The strip sits roughly one-fifth of the way down from the top of the initial content viewport, clearly separating the introductory title area from the lower, denser content sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially scrolls with the content; in combination with section-scroll-handler it can become a sticky or semi-sticky bar that pins to the top of the content area (just below the browser viewport top) once the user scrolls past the title, maintaining visibility as the user navigates between sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-scroll-handler has no visible presence but conceptually wraps or attaches to the section-navigation and all content sections (shipping-overview-section through restocking-fees-section). It occupies the same horizontal footprint as the main content container and the vertical range from just above the section-navigation down to the bottom of the last section. Its role is to define logical anchor points at the top of each content section and ensure that clicking a section-navigation link scrolls the page so that the corresponding section heading aligns neatly near the top of the viewport, with a small buffer of 16–24px above the heading for breathing room, in keeping with the page’s spacious spacing system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible behavioral layer; controls smooth scrolling to section anchors and may toggle sticky behavior on the section-navigation based on scroll position, but does not render any visible UI or occupy explicit visual space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping-returns-content-loader acts as an invisible wrapper around all Shipping & Returns content sections and the shipping-methods-loader. It shares the same horizontal alignment as the main content container, starting just below the section-navigation and extending to the bottom of the last content section. When data is loading or refreshing, it can display a lightweight, centrally aligned loading state that appears within the content column—e.g., a spinner or skeleton blocks centered horizontally at about one-third down the visible content area—while maintaining the overall vertical spacing pattern. Once loading completes, this loader collapses to zero visual height, letting the content sections occupy the space seamlessly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits inline in the content flow immediately under the section-navigation. Temporarily overlays or replaces its child sections with a loading indicator when needed, then becomes visually absent; it does not fix or float relative to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping-overview-section is the first major block in the main content column, positioned directly under the shipping-returns-content-loader (once loaded) and aligned to the left edge of the inner content container. A vertical gap of 32–40px below the section-navigation separates it clearly. The section consists of a left-aligned H2 heading (“Shipping Overview” or similar) spanning roughly the left 60–70% of the container, followed by 1–3 short paragraphs of body text with about 16–24px line spacing. The text column typically occupies about two-thirds of the container width, leaving comfortable white margins on both sides; the right third remains visually open, echoing the breathable midsection of the mockup. Bottom spacing of 32–48px clearly delineates the end of this section before the next one.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and static in the main vertical flow; it scrolls normally with the page and does not change position or behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The free-shipping-section appears directly below the shipping-overview-section, separated by a vertical gap of roughly 32–40px. It remains aligned to the left within the same centered content container. A bold or semi-bold H2 heading introduces the free shipping criteria, again spanning about 60–70% of the container’s width, followed by concise bullet points or short paragraphs. If bullets are used, they form a single column with clear 8–16px vertical spacing between items, keeping the density moderate. The section’s content keeps roughly the same width as the overview text, maintaining a unified column; the right side remains open, preserving the airy feeling. Bottom spacing of about 32–48px separates it from the delivery-areas-section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline section within the vertical content stack with no special scrolling behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery-areas-section follows the free-shipping-section, sitting 32–40px beneath it in the same left-aligned content column. Its heading introduces where shipping is available (e.g., cities, states, regions). The body can be structured as short paragraphs plus a simple list or grouped labels; all content remains within roughly the left 70% of the container width, mirroring previous sections. If multiple regions or zones are presented, they are arranged as a vertically stacked list rather than multiple columns, to preserve the single-column, low-density feel. The bottom of this section has 32–48px of spacing before the shipping-methods-loader and shipping-methods-section appear, signaling a transition into more structured, data-heavy content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and static, scrolling naturally with the rest of the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping-methods-loader occupies the vertical space immediately below the delivery-areas-section and directly above the shipping-methods-section. Horizontally, it matches the width and left alignment of the main content container. When the shipping methods data is being retrieved or updated, this loader may temporarily display a skeleton table or a centered spinner with a label (“Loading shipping methods…”) that fills roughly the same vertical area as the eventual table, starting about 16–24px beneath the section heading area reserved for shipping-methods-section. Once data is loaded, the loader’s visual content disappears and its height collapses, allowing the shipping-methods-section table to take its place seamlessly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the content flow; it toggles visibility and height based on loading state but does not alter its alignment or position relative to other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping-methods-section is a data-table block that occupies the same left-aligned main content column but feels denser, analogous to the product grid region in the mockup. It begins with a left-aligned H2 heading (“Shipping Methods & Rates” or similar) aligned with previous section headings, followed by 16–24px of whitespace before the table itself. The table spans a larger width than the text-only sections—about 80–90% of the inner container width—while still respecting side margins, making it visually more prominent. Columns (e.g., Method, Delivery Time, Cost, Notes) are arranged in a single horizontally scrolling row, but on desktop they usually fit without scroll; each cell has 16px horizontal and 12–16px vertical padding to keep the content legible yet compact. Rows are stacked vertically, with subtle row dividers aligned to the 8px grid, providing a rhythm similar to product cards in a grid. The table’s block height can extend across much of the lower viewport, making this section the densest visual area on the page. A 40–48px gap below the table separates it clearly from the returns-policy-section that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline block that scrolls with the content. It may horizontally scroll within its own area on narrower viewports but does not fix or float relative to the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The returns-policy-section appears below the shipping-methods-section, separated by a generous 40–48px gap to give visual relief after the dense table. It reverts to the simpler text-section pattern: a left-aligned H2 (“Returns Policy”) aligned with the column of previous headings, followed by 2–4 explanatory paragraphs. Text again occupies roughly the left 60–70% of the container, with increased breathing room between paragraphs (16–24px). Important rules or timelines can be highlighted using short bullet lists, but still within a single column. This section visually feels like a calm, explanatory block after the more data-heavy table, similar in density to the shipping-overview-section at the top. Another 32–40px of vertical space below this section separates it from the restocking-fees-section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and static, part of the main vertical scroll flow with no special behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The restocking-fees-section is the final primary content block, positioned 32–40px below the returns-policy-section in the same left-aligned column. The section starts with a heading (“Restocking Fees”) aligned with previous headings, followed by concise text that may include a short bullet list or a small table-like layout for different fee percentages, but still kept in a single-column format. The width follows the same pattern as other text sections (about 60–70% of the container), ensuring consistent line lengths. Because this section is informational but relatively narrow in scope, it should be visually compact, with a slightly smaller vertical footprint than the returns-policy-section. A generous 40–56px gap below it sets apart the bottom navigation area where the back-to-checkout-link is placed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline section that scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping-faq-link is presented as a small, secondary text link placed near the top of the main content area, within the same horizontal band as the section-navigation but aligned to the right. After the H1 and the section-navigation row are rendered, the link appears in the rightmost portion of that band or just beneath it, occupying a narrow inline area (~1–2 grid columns wide) with right alignment to the container edge. It sits with about 8–16px horizontal spacing from the contact-returns-link, forming a pair of subtle utility links. Vertically, it is centered relative to the section-navigation’s height or placed 8–12px below it, ensuring it doesn’t visually compete with the main navigation links but is discoverable for users seeking extra help. It uses standard body-size type with understated styling (e.g., simple underline on hover), reinforcing its role as secondary navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and static; it scrolls away with the top content and does not remain fixed, staying within the same relative vertical position as the section-navigation at page load.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-returns-link sits adjacent to the shipping-faq-link within the same top utility band of the main content. It is right-aligned within the content container, positioned either immediately to the left or right of the shipping-faq-link with a 16–24px gap between them, forming a small cluster of text-based help actions. Like its sibling link, it uses small, unobtrusive typography and minimal styling, ensuring that the main focus remains on the section-navigation and page title. Vertically, it is aligned with the baseline of the shipping-faq-link, and together they occupy a compact horizontal strip just above the first content section (shipping-overview-section) and below or alongside the section-navigation. This makes it easy for users arriving from support contexts to spot a direct contact option without overwhelming the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline link; scrolls with the content and holds its position relative to the section-navigation and top of the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-checkout-link is positioned at the bottom of the main content column as a prominent exit path. After the restocking-fees-section concludes and a vertical gap of about 40–56px, this link appears left-aligned with the rest of the content, occupying a small horizontal area (~2–3 grid columns) near the bottom-left corner of the container. It is styled more prominently than the top utility links—visually closer to a primary text button or ghost button, with stronger color contrast (e.g., brand blue outline or text using the accent color) and slightly larger font size. It sits above the page footer, separated by another 32–48px of whitespace, ensuring it doesn’t blend into footer navigation. This placement echoes the way the product grid extends downward in the mockup, with a clear action point as the user reaches the end of the content, guiding them back to the checkout flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical flow, appearing only when the user scrolls to the bottom of the content; it does not stick to the viewport but remains easily reachable at the natural end of the page.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Page that dynamically displays the privacy policy content, explains data handling practices, exposes user rights, and provides a dedicated privacy contact path while keeping access to the cart summary.

core_features: Fetch and display the full privacy policy (including sections on data collection, usage, retention, protection measures, and user rights) using getPrivacyPolicyContent.
Display the privacy policy as separate, addressable sections with an optional in-page navigation that can be controlled via URL parameters.
Show the last updated date of the privacy policy using data from getPrivacyPolicyContent.
Provide a dedicated 'Contact us about privacy' action that navigates to contact.html with the topic=privacy URL parameter and optional context of the currently viewed section.
Display a compact cart summary in the header (item count and subtotal) using getCartSummary, allowing users to keep track of their cart while reading the policy.
Support deep-linking to a specific policy section and reflecting section changes in the URL so that links can be shared/bookmarked.
user_workflows:
Read the privacy policy:
1. User opens privacy.html (via footer link or direct URL).
2. On page load, the page controller calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. The returned last_updated value is displayed as the policy's last update date.
4. The returned sections array is rendered as an ordered list of sections.
5. If a section URL parameter is present, the page scrolls to and highlights that section; otherwise, the first section is shown as the default focus.
6. The user scrolls through or uses the section navigation (if present) to read specific sections.

Navigate directly to a specific policy section via URL:
1. A user opens privacy.html with an optional query parameter section (e.g., privacy.html?section={sectionIdentifier}).
2. The page parses the section parameter and waits for getPrivacyPolicyContent to resolve.
3. Once sections are loaded, the page attempts to match the section parameter to a section identifier derived from the API response (for example, an index or a slug computed from the section title).
4. If a matching section is found, the page automatically scrolls to that section and highlights it in the section list and table of contents.
5. If no matching section is found, the page falls back to the default section (e.g., the first section) without erroring.

Use the privacy contact link:
1. While viewing the privacy policy, the user clicks a 'Contact us about privacy' link or button.
2. The click handler checks whether a specific section is currently focused (based on the last interacted or visible section).
3. The handler constructs a URL for contact.html including the topic=privacy parameter and may append an additional parameter that identifies the originating section (e.g., section={currentSectionIdentifier}).
4. The browser navigates to contact.html with the constructed query string.
5. The contact page can use topic=privacy (and section if present) to pre-select the topic or pre-fill contextual information.

View cart information from the privacy page header:
1. When privacy.html loads, the page controller calls getCartSummary in parallel with getPrivacyPolicyContent.
2. The header cart summary component receives item_count, subtotal, and currency from getCartSummary.
3. The header displays a cart icon, the current cart item_count, and the subtotal with the appropriate currency.
4. When the user clicks the cart summary, the page navigates to the cart page (e.g., cart.html) without modifying any privacy page parameters.

Share a link to the currently viewed policy section:
1. User navigates or scrolls to a specific privacy policy section.
2. As the active section changes (via click in the table of contents or scroll detection), the page updates the section query parameter in the URL using the section’s identifier (e.g., without reloading the page).
3. The user copies the page URL from the browser address bar.
4. When another user opens that URL, the deep-link workflow is triggered and the corresponding section becomes focused automatically.
interactions: On page load, automatically call getPrivacyPolicyContent and getCartSummary and render the page only after responses or show a loading state while waiting.
Click on a section in the table of contents navigates to the corresponding section within the document and updates the URL section parameter to match the selected section identifier.
Scrolling through the policy content can update the active section indicator in the table of contents and synchronize the URL section parameter with the currently in-view section.
Click on 'Contact us about privacy' triggers navigation to contact.html with topic=privacy plus any additional context parameters derived from the current section and/or URL state.
Click on the cart summary in the header navigates the user to the cart page (e.g., cart.html) while preserving the browser history so they can navigate back to the privacy policy.

If either getPrivacyPolicyContent or getCartSummary fails, an error state is displayed for the respective component (policy content area or cart summary) with an option to retry by re-triggering the corresponding interface call.
page_functionality.state_logic: The privacy.html page uses query parameters to manage and reflect state, without relying on localStorage. The primary parameter is section, which represents the currently focused or requested policy section. The value of section is derived from the data returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent (for example, an index or a slug computed from the section title). On initial load, the page controller parses the URL. If section is present, it is stored in in-memory page state and used after the sections are loaded to scroll to and highlight the requested section. If section is absent or invalid, the default behavior is to focus on the first section in the returned list. When the user navigates between sections via the table of contents or as the visible section changes on scroll, the page updates the section parameter in the URL using the History API in a way that does not cause a full reload. Outgoing navigation to contact.html always includes topic=privacy as required and may also include the current section parameter to maintain context (for example, contact.html?topic=privacy&section={currentSectionIdentifier}). Cart state is not stored or modified by this page; it is read-only via getCartSummary and any navigation to cart.html does not affect URL parameters on privacy.html.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, global page state, and communication between the policy content components and the header cart summary.
Displays a compact summary of the current cart (item count and subtotal) in the page header and allows navigation to the cart page.
Shows general metadata about the privacy policy, such as the last updated date and the privacy contact email provided by the API.
Renders a navigable list of privacy policy sections so users can jump directly to specific topics. Synchronizes with the active section and updates/reads the URL section parameter.

Displays all privacy policy sections returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent in order, handles scrolling to a specific section based on URL or TOC interaction, and informs other components of the currently active section.

Provides a dedicated link or button for privacy-related inquiries. Uses the privacy_contact_email for display context if needed and navigates to contact.html with topic=privacy and the current section identifier when clicked.
Handles and displays errors related to loading the privacy policy content and provides a way to retry the getPrivacyPolicyContent call without reloading the entire page.
Handles and displays errors related to loading the cart summary and provides a way to retry the getCartSummary call.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid typical of WooCommerce/WordPress themes, with content centered in a fixed-width container inside a full-bleed header.
layout.layout_pattern: Reuse the e‑commerce category page structure: global header at the very top (black utility strip + yellow main header with logo/nav/cart), followed by the full‑width search/category bar, then a single breadcrumb row centered in the main content container. Beneath that, keep a generous vertical gap leading into a large, centered H1 'Privacy Policy'. Immediately below the title we place policy‑specific controls in the same band where the mockup showed the horizontal category filter: a compact row combining the privacy policy meta info (left/center) and a clear privacy contact link (right) plus the horizontal table of contents row. Below this band, the main scrolling area is the vertical list of policy sections, occupying the lower half of the viewport and beyond. The header cart summary remains in the top‑right of the header, and any chat widget / WhatsApp FAB continue to overlay on the right, independent of the privacy content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Reuse the e‑commerce category page structure: global header at the very top (black utility strip + yellow main header with logo/nav/cart), followed by the full‑width search/category bar, then a single breadcrumb row centered in the main content container. Beneath that, keep a generous vertical gap leading into a large, centered H1 'Privacy Policy'. Immediately below the title we place policy‑specific controls in the same band where the mockup showed the horizontal category filter: a compact row combining the privacy policy meta info (left/center) and a clear privacy contact link (right) plus the horizontal table of contents row. Below this band, the main scrolling area is the vertical list of policy sections, occupying the lower half of the viewport and beyond. The header cart summary remains in the top‑right of the header, and any chat widget / WhatsApp FAB continue to overlay on the right, independent of the privacy content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain the mockup’s vertical proportions: the header stack (black bar + yellow nav + search bar) fills roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport. The mid‑page framing area (breadcrumb, whitespace, H1, policy meta + TOC band) takes another ~20–25%, with deliberately generous gaps (24–48px) between each layer to keep the page light. The lower 50–60% is dedicated to the policy text itself, scrolling vertically. Horizontally, all privacy content (breadcrumb, title, meta, TOC, sections) sits inside a centered fixed‑width container (~1100–1200px or about 70–80% of the viewport), aligned to a 12‑column grid with comfortable left/right margins. The cart summary and any overlay chat panel occupy compact areas along the right edge, never exceeding ~25–30% of the width.
layout_strategies.content_density: Follow the low‑density, breathable pattern at the top: sparse content with strong separation between breadcrumb, title, and the meta/TOC band, using 24–48px vertical gaps. The policy sections area is denser, like the product grid zone in the mockup: section headings and paragraphs follow closer vertical rhythm (16–24px between related elements, 32–48px between sections), but still sit on the 8px spacing grid so the page feels structured rather than cramped. Meta information and error states use concise blocks with clear padding (12–16px vertical, 16–24px horizontal). Overall, the page reads as a clean legal detail page with plenty of whitespace around major elements.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the Z‑pattern: the eye starts at the top‑left with the logo/brand in the header, moves right across the yellow navigation to the cart summary area, then drops diagonally down to the left side of the search bar and breadcrumb. From there, it travels horizontally across the search/category bar, then down to the centered H1. The gaze then sweeps right across the meta + privacy contact link + horizontal TOC band before moving downward into the vertically flowing policy sections. The cart summary in the header and any right‑side chat/FAB elements remain secondary focal points that gently pull attention to the right edge without overpowering the main content.

layout.overall_description: The Privacy Policy page visually mirrors a product category page, but replaces the product grid with structured legal content. A full‑width header stretches across the top: a slim black utility strip above a bold yellow navigation bar holding the logo on the left and account/cart actions on the right. Directly beneath, a full‑width band contains a left‑aligned “Browse Categories” trigger and a large central search field, both inside a centered content container, giving the top of the page a familiar e‑commerce feel. Below this header stack, the main content column is centered with wide left and right margins. A single breadcrumb row sits near the top of this column, followed by a comfortable vertical gap that leads into a large, centered “Privacy Policy” title. Another generous gap opens into a horizontal band dedicated to policy controls: subtle meta information sits near the left/center, while a distinctly styled privacy contact link aligns toward the right and a horizontal table of contents spans across as text links. This band visually replaces the product category filter from the mockup while serving a legal‑page purpose. Below, the page transitions into the denser content region: a vertically scrolling list of policy sections with clear headings and paragraphs, left‑aligned within the main column. Throughout, the layout remains calm and regular, with all text aligned to a 12‑column grid and spacing strictly following 8px multiples. Overlaid on top of this content, the cart summary remains accessible from the top‑right of the header, and a right‑side chat panel and bottom‑right floating button (if present) sit above the content layers, echoing the interactive affordances seen in the original mockup without disturbing the linear reading flow of the policy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller is an invisible structural layer that spans the entire viewport, orchestrating the standard e‑commerce frame and the privacy content within it. It anchors the global header at the top, the full‑width search/category bar directly below, and then a centered main content container that stretches vertically from just under the search bar down to the fold and beyond. Within this container, it defines a vertical stack: breadcrumb row at the top of the container, followed by a 32–40px gap, then the centered H1 title, another 24–32px gap, then the meta/TOC/contact band, followed by another 32–48px gap before the policy sections list begins. It also reserves a narrow, non‑interactive margin on the far right so that any overlayed chat panel and floating buttons can sit without obscuring key text. When errors occur, the controller swaps out the normal content area (meta + TOC + section list) for the appropriate error states while preserving the header and search structure above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full‑page structural wrapper, static in the document flow; coordinates placement of header, content column, and error states without itself being visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart summary widget is positioned in the top‑right portion of the yellow main header bar, aligned horizontally with the primary navigation items. Visually, it occupies roughly the rightmost 10–15% of the header width, consisting of a cart icon and possibly a compact item count badge. On desktop, when the user interacts with it, a small dropdown or slide‑down panel appears directly below the icon, aligned to the right edge of the main content container and extending downward into the page by about 220–320px in height and 280–340px in width. This panel overlays the top of the privacy content without permanently shifting it, similar to a mini‑cart on an e‑commerce page. The widget and its panel stay visually separate from the main privacy content column beneath, thanks to a clear shadow and background contrast, and maintain generous interior padding (16–24px) for clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The icon is effectively sticky within the fixed header region; the summary panel is a floating overlay positioned relative to the header’s right edge, appearing on demand and dismissing without affecting the underlying content layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy meta information block is placed within the main content column just below the H1 title. The H1 is centered; immediately beneath it, after roughly 16–24px of vertical space, the meta block appears as a single horizontal line or compact stack of text (e.g., 'Last updated' date, data controller info), aligned left within the central column but visually centered under the title due to symmetrical margins. It spans about 60–70% of the content width, starting slightly inset from the left grid edge to keep it visually light. The text is small and subtle, with minimal height, so it does not compete with the title. There is a further 12–16px gap between this meta block and the beginning of the table of contents/controls row below, maintaining the breathable vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline within the vertical flow between the title and the TOC; it scrolls away naturally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The table of contents occupies the same visual tier that the category filter held in the original mockup. Positioned directly below the meta information (after a 12–16px gap), it spans almost the full width of the central content column (about 80–90% of the container, centered). The TOC is rendered as a single horizontal band of section links, starting slightly left of center and flowing to the right, wrapping to a second row if necessary. Each link has comfortable horizontal padding (12–16px) and vertical padding (8–12px), with equal spacing between items to maintain the calm, flat design. A subtle bottom rule or background tint can separate this band from the white page, echoing the visual treatment of the category filter bar. Beneath the TOC band there is a clear 24–32px vertical gap before the first policy section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline in the content flow by default; on larger viewports it can optionally become sticky to the top of the viewport once scrolled into position, staying just below the main header so users can jump between sections as they scroll, without altering the underlying grid alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section list forms the main body of the page and begins immediately below the TOC band, after a 24–32px whitespace buffer. It is left‑aligned within the same central content column and spans approximately 70–75% of the container width, leaving generous white margins on both sides to maintain readability and echo the product grid’s left‑anchored feel. Each section consists of a heading and one or more paragraphs/bullets, with 8–12px spacing between heading and first paragraph, 12–16px between paragraphs, and 32–48px between sections, all on an 8px grid. The content runs vertically and scrolls below the fold, occupying the lower half of the viewport and beyond. Anchors assigned to each section align with the TOC links, so clicking a TOC item scrolls the corresponding heading close to the top of the visible content area, just under the header and any sticky TOC.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically scrolling content block; it expands as needed with the amount of policy text, always respecting the fixed column width and center alignment of the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy contact link is positioned near the top of the main content area to serve as a clear action without disrupting the calm layout. On desktop, it appears in the same horizontal band as the TOC and meta information: aligned to the right edge of the central content column, visually opposite the left‑leaning meta text and TOC links. Practically, this means that under the centered H1, the meta sits just below and slightly to the left, the TOC links flow across the middle portion, and on the far right of that band, a compact but visually distinct button or emphasized link (in brand blue or outlined with brand color) reads 'Contact our Privacy Team' or similar. It occupies roughly 10–15% of the content width and has comfortable padding (8–12px vertical, 16–20px horizontal). There is a small 8–12px clearance above and below it to avoid crowding the TOC links while staying visually grouped with them as part of the page’s control strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline control within the meta/TOC band; it scrolls with the content. On smaller viewports it can drop below the TOC links, still right‑aligned within the column, maintaining its clear call‑to‑action role without becoming a floating element that competes with global chat/FAB buttons.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the privacy policy content fails to load, the error state appears in place of the meta/TOC/section content block, keeping the familiar framing above (header, search bar, breadcrumb, H1) intact. It is centered within the main content column, starting roughly where the TOC band would normally begin. The error card spans about 60% of the column width, centered horizontally, with generous padding (24–32px all around) and a clear message plus optional retry action. Vertically, there is a 24–32px gap above the card (under the title) and similar spacing below before the end of the visible content, preserving the open feel. This ensures the page still looks clean and structured even when showing an error, rather than collapsing into a cramped layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as an inline replacement for the meta/TOC/sections within the main content column; it scrolls as a normal block and is shown or hidden by the page controller depending on the data state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: If the cart summary cannot be loaded, the error state is localized to the cart interaction area in the header so it does not interfere with the privacy content. When the user opens the cart summary, instead of showing line items, the dropdown/slide‑down panel under the cart icon is filled with an error message. This error panel keeps the same width and position as the normal cart summary (aligned with the header’s right edge, about 280–340px wide, 160–220px tall), with inner padding of 16–24px and concise text indicating the problem plus any retry or link to the full cart page. No extra space is added to the main content below; the panel temporarily overlays the upper part of the privacy page just as the normal mini‑cart does.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floating overlay tied to the header-cart-summary component; it appears only when the cart panel is invoked and behaves like a modal dropdown, overlaying but not shifting the underlying layout.

name: Terms & Conditions
title: Terms & Conditions
description: Page that retrieves and displays the website and sales terms, with basic navigation, search, cart summary, and context-aware links to checkout and contact.

core_features: Fetch and display Terms & Conditions content, including a last-updated indicator and multiple sections, using getTermsAndConditionsContent.
Display a header cart summary (item count and subtotal) using getCartSummary so users can see their cart status while reviewing terms.
Provide in-page navigation between terms sections (e.g., via a section list or anchors) synchronized with URL parameters for deep-linking.
Support a context-aware 'Back to checkout' link when the page is opened during an order flow, navigating to checkout.html.
Provide a 'Contact us about terms' link that navigates to contact.html with topic=terms for questions about specific clauses.
Allow users to search within the loaded terms text on the client side (no additional API calls), highlighting or filtering matching sections.
Support deep-linking to a specific section via URL parameters so external pages can link directly to a subsection of the terms.
Handle loading and error states for both getTermsAndConditionsContent and getCartSummary without relying on any hardcoded text content.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: General visitor reads terms
1. User clicks the 'Terms & Conditions' link in the site footer or navigates directly to terms.html.
2. On page load, the controller component parses URL parameters (e.g., context, section).
3. The page triggers getTermsAndConditionsContent to retrieve last_updated and sections.
4. The page in parallel triggers getCartSummary to retrieve item_count, subtotal, and currency for the header.
5. When terms content is loaded, the page renders the list of sections and, if a section parameter is present, scrolls to and highlights that section.
6. User scrolls or uses the in-page navigation to move between sections.
7. User optionally uses the search field to filter or highlight text within the loaded sections.
8. User can click 'Contact us about terms' to navigate to contact.html?topic=terms in a new page or the same tab.

Workflow 2: User reviewing terms during checkout
1. User is in the checkout flow on checkout.html.
2. From checkout, user clicks a link (outside this page) that opens terms.html with context=checkout (for example, in a new tab or the same tab).
3. On load, the page parses context=checkout and calls getTermsAndConditionsContent and getCartSummary.
4. The terms content is displayed; since context=checkout is present, the page also displays a 'Back to checkout' action.
5. User reads sections, optionally jumps between sections or uses the search field.
6. When done, user clicks the 'Back to checkout' control.
7. The page navigates to checkout.html, allowing the checkout flow to continue (any acceptance of terms is handled on checkout.html, not on this page).

Workflow 3: Deep-link to a specific section and ask a question
1. An internal user or system shares a link such as terms.html?section={index} (where {index} is derived from the order of sections in getTermsAndConditionsContent) to highlight a specific part of the terms.
2. Recipient opens the link; the page parses the section parameter and loads terms content.
3. After sections are rendered, the page scrolls to the section at the index specified by the section parameter and visually marks it as active.
4. User reads the highlighted section; if they have a question, they click 'Contact us about terms'.
5. The page navigates to contact.html?topic=terms, where the user can describe their question and, if needed, reference the section title manually.

Workflow 4: User checks cart status while reading terms
1. User arrives on terms.html from any entry point.
2. On load, the page calls getCartSummary in parallel with getTermsAndConditionsContent.
3. When getCartSummary resolves, the header cart summary component shows the current item_count and subtotal with the currency from the response.
4. User can click the cart icon or summary (navigation logic defined elsewhere) or remain on the terms page.
5. If the cart changes in another tab, the summary can be refreshed via a manual refresh action or a timed refresh configured on this page (using the same getCartSummary interface without additional APIs).
interactions:
On page load (onLoad):
- Parse URL parameters such as context and section.
- Trigger getTermsAndConditionsContent.
- Trigger getCartSummary.
- After terms are loaded, if a valid section parameter is present, scroll to and highlight the corresponding section.

Section navigation click (onClick on a section navigation item):
- Update the active section in the page state.
- Update the URL section parameter using history APIs without reloading the page (e.g., terms.html?section={index}[&context=...]).
- Scroll smoothly to the selected section in the document.

Search within terms (onSubmit and onInput in search-form):
- On text input change, update a search query state.
- On form submit, iterate over the loaded sections (title and content) to find matches.
- Optionally filter the visible section list to only matching sections or highlight matching text within sections.
- Do not call any additional APIs; use only the already-loaded terms content.

'Back to checkout' action (onClick when context=checkout):
- When the button or link is clicked, navigate to checkout.html.
- Preserve no additional parameters, as per the defined outgoing connection (navigation contracts).

'Contact us about terms' link (onClick):
- When clicked, navigate to contact.html?topic=terms.
- If other, unrelated URL parameters are present on the terms page, they are not forwarded unless globally handled outside this page’s defined behavior.

Cart summary refresh (onClick optional):
- User can click a refresh control associated with the cart summary (if provided).
- When clicked, call getCartSummary again and update the summary display with the new values.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameter–based state logic:

- context parameter:
  - Accepted values are treated generically as strings, but this page specifically reacts to the value 'checkout'.
  - If context=checkout:
    - The terms page displays a 'Back to checkout' control.
    - The control's onClick handler navigates to checkout.html.
  - If context is absent or has any other value, the 'Back to checkout' control is not shown.

- section parameter:
  - Interpreted as a zero-based or one-based numeric index (implementation-defined but consistent) referencing the position of the section in the sections array returned by getTermsAndConditionsContent.
  - On initial load, after the sections array is rendered, if section corresponds to a valid index within the array:
    - The page scrolls to the associated section.
    - The associated section is marked active in the UI (e.g., highlighted in the navigation list and/or section header).
  - If the index is invalid or missing, the page shows the first section as the default active section without forcing a scroll.
  - When a user clicks a section in the in-page navigation, the page updates the section parameter in the URL (using history.replaceState or history.pushState) to match the clicked section index without causing a full reload.

- Combination of parameters:
  - Both context and section can be present at the same time. For example, terms.html?context=checkout&section=2 will show the checkout-specific navigation and scroll to the section at index 2.
  - When updating the section parameter from navigation clicks, the context parameter (if present) is preserved.

- Loading and error state:
  - While getTermsAndConditionsContent is pending, the page state reflects a 'loading' mode for the main content component. No static placeholder text is hardcoded; instead, generic loading indicators are used that do not depend on specific content.
  - If getTermsAndConditionsContent fails, the state includes an error flag. The UI can display a generic error message that does not reference specific terms content.
  - While getCartSummary is pending, the cart summary component may show a generic loading state or hide values until data arrives, without using hardcoded cart values.

- No localStorage:
  - The page does not persist any state to localStorage or similar browser storage. All state (active section, context, search query) is held in memory and, where needed for navigation or deep-linking, represented via URL parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, high-level state management, and inter-component communication for the Terms & Conditions page.
Displays a compact cart summary in the page header, including item count and subtotal in the correct currency, using data from getCartSummary.
Requests terms content via getTermsAndConditionsContent, exposes loading and error states, and passes the last_updated and sections data to child components.
Shows the last updated date or value for the terms, using the last_updated property from getTermsAndConditionsContent.
Renders the list of terms sections (title and content) returned from getTermsAndConditionsContent, with each section having a stable index-based identifier for navigation and deep-linking.
Provides a navigation list of the terms sections, allows users to jump to a given section, and keeps its active state in sync with the current URL section parameter and scroll position.
Allows users to enter a search query to find matches within the loaded terms sections, updating the visible or highlighted sections based on the search term.
Displays context-sensitive actions based on URL parameters, including a 'Back to checkout' link when context=checkout and a persistent 'Contact us about terms' link.
Provides a control to trigger the browser's print dialog for the current Terms & Conditions view, without altering any data or making API calls.
Displays error messages related to loading terms content or cart summary in a generic way, driven by error state from the data loader components.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid typical of WooCommerce/WordPress themes, with content centered in a fixed-width container inside a full-bleed header.
layout.layout_pattern: Reuse the product-listing page’s vertical rhythm: global header at the very top (outside this spec), then a full-width utility/search band, followed by a slim meta/breadcrumb-style row, then a generous gap leading into a large centered page title, then a single-row horizontal section navigation (like category filters), and below that the main terms content in a left-aligned text column. Supporting elements (cart summary, contextual actions, print) sit on the right edge of the main content band to echo the cart/account presence in the mockup header. Loader and error states take over the main content region without disturbing the global header or chat widgets.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Reuse the product-listing page’s vertical rhythm: global header at the very top (outside this spec), then a full-width utility/search band, followed by a slim meta/breadcrumb-style row, then a generous gap leading into a large centered page title, then a single-row horizontal section navigation (like category filters), and below that the main terms content in a left-aligned text column. Supporting elements (cart summary, contextual actions, print) sit on the right edge of the main content band to echo the cart/account presence in the mockup header. Loader and error states take over the main content region without disturbing the global header or chat widgets.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header (black utility bar + yellow main header) retains the top ~15–20% of the viewport. Immediately below, the search band with the terms search and cart summary occupies another ~8–10% of initial viewport height. The meta row plus the generous whitespace, page title, and section navigation together occupy roughly another 15–20%, mirroring the open mid-page area in the mockup. The remaining ~50–60% is dedicated to scrolling legal content: a central fixed-width container (~1140–1200px) with the text column taking about two-thirds of that width and the remaining third acting as breathing space and room for right-aligned actions and the overlayed chat/WhatsApp widgets.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low visual density in the upper half: the search band, last-updated meta, and the title each get ample vertical padding (24–32px) and clear separation. The section navigation is compact but not cramped, with 16–24px vertical padding. In the main terms text, paragraphs and section headings are reasonably dense for reading but still respect 8–16px spacing increments to feel breathable, not legalese-wall. Utility items like cart summary, contextual actions, and print remain visually light and spaced out so they do not clutter the reading area.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Keep the Z-pattern from the mockup. The eye goes from the top-left logo and black bar, across the yellow header to the right-side cart/account icons, then diagonally down to the center-left of the page where the terms search and last-updated info sit. From there the gaze moves to the large, centered 'Terms & Conditions' title, then sweeps left-to-right across the horizontal section navigation and right-aligned contextual actions/print button. Finally, attention flows downward into the left-aligned text column. Throughout, the floating blue chat panel and bottom-right WhatsApp FAB sit on the right edge as secondary focal points that do not interrupt the primary reading flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing full-width black utility bar and yellow main header, the Terms & Conditions page begins with a full-width horizontal band echoing the product search area. Inside a centered, fixed-width container, a wide search field dedicated to terms appears slightly left of center while a compact cart summary block aligns to the far right of the same row. Directly beneath, a thin meta row displays the last-updated information left-aligned, spanning most of the content width. After a generous vertical gap, the page title 'Terms & Conditions' appears as a prominent, centered H1 within the same container. Another comfortable gap leads to a single-line horizontal section navigation: a row of text-based links for different legal sections styled like category filters, roughly centered across the page. On the right end of this row (within the same horizontal band), subtle primary actions like 'Go to checkout' and 'Contact us', along with a smaller print icon-button, align to the right edge of the content column. Below this band, the main legal text flows in a left-aligned column of comfortable width, beginning near the left margin of the container and extending downward. Sections are clearly separated with headings, but the overall density is moderate to keep reading comfortable. When data is loading, a semi-opaque overlay with a spinner covers the title, navigation, and text areas while leaving the global header and chat/WhatsApp widgets visible. If an error occurs, a prominent alert card appears in the same central column where the text would be, framed by generous white space. The floating right-side chat panel and bottom-right WhatsApp button sit above this layout, visually anchored to the right, without affecting the grid alignment of the terms content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller acts as the structural wrapper for all terms-related content below the global header. It spans the full browser width but constrains its inner content to a centered fixed-width container (approximately 1140–1200px) aligned to the same grid as the product page. A top margin equivalent to the combined height of the black utility bar and yellow header ensures the terms layout begins just below the header. Within this controller, components are stacked vertically in the following order with clear separation: the search band (containing the terms search form and the header cart summary), the last-updated meta row, a generous whitespace block, the H1 page title, a small gap, the section navigation row (with contextual actions and print button aligned within it), and finally the scrolling terms section list. The loader and error notice appear within this same container, occupying the central content region. Horizontal alignment and spacing of all nested components snap to the 12-column grid and 8px spacing system established by the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container in normal flow; defines internal padding (24–32px top and bottom for each major section) and central alignment for all child components, but does not itself scroll independently of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart summary appears on the first content band directly under the yellow main header, sharing a row with the terms search form. Within the central container, this row spans the full width. The cart summary aligns flush to the far right edge of the container, occupying roughly 2–3 columns on the 12-column grid (about 15–20% of the container width), and is vertically centered in the band. It presents a concise line such as 'Cart: 3 items · ₹12,500' in a compact, flat style matching the header cart area. The vertical padding of the band is about 16–24px, giving the cart summary breathing room without feeling bulky. To its left, across the remaining ~80–85% width, the terms search form sits so that the row maintains a clear left-to-right hierarchy: search first, quick cart status last. There is at least a 16px gap between the right edge of the search field and the left edge of the cart summary to preserve clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in a fixed-height horizontal band; remains in place as part of the page flow and scrolls off screen with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content loader overlays the main terms content area when the page is retrieving terms data. It is visually confined to the central content container managed by the page controller, starting just below the search/meta region and extending down to cover the area where the title, section navigation, and section list normally appear. Horizontally, it matches the width of the text column (roughly 8–10 of 12 columns) and is centered within the container, leaving the outer margins and global header visible. The loader uses a semi-opaque white or very light overlay with a centered spinner or progress indicator, so the underlying structure is hinted but non-interactive. Vertical padding around the spinner (32–48px) keeps it visually centered in the initial viewport area, echoing the calm spacing of the mockup’s mid-page whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned overlay anchored within the page controller: uses absolute positioning relative to the content container so it floats above the title, navigation, and section list, intercepting interaction until loading completes, while scrolling behavior remains tied to the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The last-updated banner sits directly beneath the search band (containing the search form and cart summary), occupying a slim horizontal row across the central container. It is left-aligned, beginning at the left margin of the main text column and extending across approximately 8–10 columns, with the text reading like a subtle breadcrumb or meta label (e.g., 'Last updated: 12 Jan 2026'). The vertical height is modest, around 24–32px in total, with 8–12px padding above and below the text. There is at least 16px of vertical space separating it from the search band above and about 32–40px of whitespace below it before the large page title, mirroring the mockup’s small breadcrumb row followed by a generous gap. The styling is understated so it provides context without competing with the title that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element in the vertical flow; scrolls naturally with the content and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms section list forms the main reading area and starts immediately below the section navigation and the contextual actions/print row.
